parts.content: Calculate the mean of the data set.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the sum of all values: $1 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 4 + 6 + 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 36$ and divide by the number of values: $\frac{36}{10} = 3.6$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Recall the definition of mean
steps.content:
To calculate the mean (often denoted as $\bar{x}$), we use the direct formula which requires summing all the data values and dividing by the total number of values:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x}{n}$$

steps.title: Sum the data values
steps.content:
First, we calculate the sum of all numbers in the given data set:

$$\sum x = 1 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 4 + 6 + 1 + 2 + 3 + 4$$

gdc.instructions: Type the addition into the calculation bar

ti84.instructions:
Enter the sum directly on the home screen:
1 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 4 + 6 + 1 + 2 + 3 + 4

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a command line

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the data values

steps.title: Calculate the sum result
steps.content:
Adding these values together gives:

$$\sum x = 36$$

steps.title: Divide by the count
steps.content:
There are $10$ values in the data set ($n = 10$). We divide the sum by this count to find the mean:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{36}{10}$$

calculator.description: Divide the sum by the count

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the division:

$$\bar{x} = 3.6$$

This calculation is covered in **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**, specifically section SL 4.3 regarding the mean.

parts.content: Determine the median of the data set.
parts.markscheme:
- Arrange the data in ascending order: $1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6$

- Identify the middle values: Since $n = 10$ (even), the median is the average of the 5th and 6th values

- Calculate the median: $\frac{4 + 4}{2} = 4$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

<examTip>When finding the median with even number of values, remember to take the average of the middle two numbers</examTip>

steps.title: Arrange data in ascending order
steps.content:
To find the median using the manual ordering method, we first list the data points from smallest to largest.

The original set is:
$$x = \{1, 4, 5, 6, 4, 6, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

Arranged in ascending order:
$$1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6$$

steps.title: Identify the middle values
steps.content:
Count the number of data points, $n$. Here, there are 10 values ($n=10$).

Since $n$ is an even number, the median is the average of the two middle values found at positions $\frac{n}{2}$ and $\frac{n}{2} + 1$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Position 1: } & \frac{10}{2} = \text{5th value} \\ \text{Position 2: } & 5 + 1 = \text{6th value} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Locate values in ordered list
steps.content:
Looking at our ordered list, we identify the 5th and 6th numbers:

$$1, 1, 2, 3, \mathbf{4}, \mathbf{4}, 4, 5, 6, 6$$

Both the 5th and 6th values are $4$.

steps.title: Calculate the median
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the average of these two middle values:

$$\text{Median} = \frac{4 + 4}{2} = 4$$

parts.content: Find the lower and upper quartiles of the data set.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the first half of the data $\{1, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and find the median:
  $Q_1 = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the second half of the data $\{4, 4, 5, 6, 6\}$ and find the median:
  $Q_3 = 5$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Sort the data set
steps.content:
First, we arrange the data set in ascending order to locate the positions of the quartiles. There are 10 values in the original set.

Original: $$\{1, 4, 5, 6, 4, 6, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

Sorted: $$\{1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6\}$$

steps.title: Divide into halves
steps.content:
Since there is an even number of data points ($n=10$), we split the data exactly down the middle into two equal halves of 5 values each.

$$\underbrace{\{1, 1, 2, 3, 4\}}_{\text{Lower Half}} \quad \mid \quad \underbrace{\{4, 4, 5, 6, 6\}}_{\text{Upper Half}}$$ 

This concept relates to **SL 4.3** in the syllabus involving Quartiles.

steps.title: Find the Lower Quartile (Q1)
steps.content:
The lower quartile ($Q_1$) is the median of the **lower half** of the data. The lower half has 5 values, so the median is the middle (3rd) term.

Lower Half: $$\{1, 1, \mathbf{2}, 3, 4\}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_1 = 2$$

highlights.text:
Identify the first half of the data {1, 1, 2, 3, 4} and find the median:
  Q_1 = 2

steps.title: Find the Upper Quartile (Q3)
steps.content:
The upper quartile ($Q_3$) is the median of the **upper half** of the data. The upper half also has 5 values, so the median is the middle (3rd) term of this subset.

Upper Half: $$\{4, 4, \mathbf{5}, 6, 6\}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_3 = 5$$

highlights.text:
Identify the second half of the data {4, 4, 5, 6, 6} and find the median:
  Q_3 = 5

parts.content: Identify any outliers in the data set using the interquartile range (IQR).
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the interquartile range: $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 5 - 2 = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the outlier boundaries: Lower bound $= Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR} = 2 - (1.5 \times 3) = -2.5$ and Upper bound $= Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR} = 5 + (1.5 \times 3) = 9.5$

- Conclude there are no outliers since all data points lie between $-2.5$ and $9.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award full marks only if both bounds are calculated and a clear conclusion is stated</note>

steps.title: Sort the data
steps.content:
First, we arrange the data set in ascending order to find the quartiles more easily. There are 10 data points.

$$1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6$$

steps.title: Find the quartiles
steps.content:
Since there are 10 values ($n=10$), we split the data into a lower half and an upper half of 5 values each.

Lower half: $1, 1, \mathbf{2}, 3, 4$ 
Upper half: $4, 4, \mathbf{5}, 6, 6$

The lower quartile ($Q_1$) is the median of the lower half, and the upper quartile ($Q_3$) is the median of the upper half.

$$\begin{aligned} Q_1 &= 2 \\ Q_3 &= 5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the IQR
steps.content:
The interquartile range (IQR) is the difference between the upper and lower quartiles.

$$IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 5 - 2 = 3$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 5 - 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate 5 - 2

steps.title: Calculate outlier boundaries
steps.content:
Outliers are values that fall below the lower boundary or above the upper boundary. The standard formula uses $1.5 \times IQR$.

Lower boundary:
$$Q_1 - 1.5 \times IQR = 2 - 1.5(3) = 2 - 4.5 = -2.5$$

Upper boundary:
$$Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR = 5 + 1.5(3) = 5 + 4.5 = 9.5$$

gdc.instructions: Compute 2 - 1.5*3 and 5 + 1.5*3

ti84.instructions: Compute 2 - 1.5*3 and 5 + 1.5*3

steps.title: Identify outliers
steps.content:
We check if any values in our data set lie outside the interval $[-2.5, 9.5]$.

- Minimum value: $1$ (inside)
- Maximum value: $6$ (inside)

Since all data values are between $-2.5$ and $9.5$, there are **no outliers**.

steps.title: Find quartiles using GDC
steps.content:
Use the 1-Variable Statistics function on your calculator to find the first quartile ($Q_1$) and the third quartile ($Q_3$).

From the GDC output:

$$\begin{aligned} Q_1 &= 2 \\ Q_3 &= 5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the IQR
steps.content:
The interquartile range (IQR) is the difference between the third and first quartiles. This step helps define the spread of the middle 50% of the data.

$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 5 - 2 = 3$$

steps.title: Determine outlier boundaries
steps.content:
Calculate the boundaries for outliers using the standard formula: $1.5 \times \text{IQR}$ below $Q_1$ and above $Q_3$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Lower Bound} &= Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR} \\ &= 2 - (1.5 \times 3) \\ &= 2 - 4.5 \\ &= -2.5 \\ \\ \text{Upper Bound} &= Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR} \\ &= 5 + (1.5 \times 3) \\ &= 5 + 4.5 \\ &= 9.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify outliers
steps.content:
Compare the original data values to these boundaries. An outlier would be any value less than $-2.5$ or greater than $9.5$.

The data range is from $1$ to $6$. Since all data values lie within the interval $[-2.5, 9.5]$, there are no outliers.

**Conclusion:** There are no outliers.

questions.specification: 100 people were asked what is the maximum they would be willing to spend on salt ranging from $1$ dollar to $10$ dollars. The data collected has the following summary statistics: minimum value = $1$, first quartile ($Q_1$) = $2$, median ($m$) = $3$, third quartile ($Q_3$) = $4$, and maximum value = $10$.

parts.content: Draw the box plot for the given data.
parts.markscheme:
![Box Plot](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/d130206a-cf08-43a4-b328-c411366d242b.png)

- Mark the box boundaries at $Q_1 = 2$ and $Q_3 = 4$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Draw the box and mark median line at $3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Draw whiskers extending to minimum ($1$) and maximum ($10$) values <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award partial marks if box plot shows correct structure but values are incorrectly positioned</note>

steps.title: Identify the 5-number summary
steps.content:
To construct a box plot, we first list the five key statistics provided in the question specification. These five values determine the geometry of the plot.

*   **Minimum:** $1$
*   **First Quartile** $(Q_1)$: $2$
*   **Median** $(m)$: $3$
*   **Third Quartile** $(Q_3)$: $4$
*   **Maximum:** $10$

steps.title: Construct the box (IQR)
steps.content:
The central box represents the Interquartile Range (IQR). Draw a rectangular box that spans from the first quartile $(Q_1)$ to the third quartile $(Q_3)$.

*   **Left edge of box:** At $x = 2$
*   **Right edge of box:** At $x = 4$

This box contains the middle $50\%$ of the data.

steps.title: Mark the median
steps.content:
Inside the box, draw a vertical line to represent the median. This indicates the center of the dataset.

*   **Median line:** At $x = 3$

steps.title: Draw the whiskers
steps.content:
Extend horizontal lines, known as "whiskers," from the sides of the box to the minimum and maximum values. These lines show the full range of the data.

*   **Left whisker:** Extends from the box ($2$) down to the minimum value ($1$).
*   **Right whisker:** Extends from the box ($4$) up to the maximum value ($10$).

Draw small vertical marks at the ends of the whiskers to clearly indicate the endpoints.

steps.title: Final check
steps.content:
Ensure your plot has a linear horizontal scale. The distance from $1$ to $2$ should be visually similar to the distance from $2$ to $3$, but the distance to the maximum ($10$) will be much longer, showing that the data is skewed to the right.

Your final plot should look like this:
*   Whisker end at $1$
*   Box start at $2$
*   Line inside box at $3$
*   Box end at $4$
*   Whisker end at $10$

parts.content: Interpret the box plot in the context of the data.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies that the central $50\%$ of the data lies between $2$ and $4$ (interquartile range) <marks>A1</marks>

- Correctly interprets median of $3$ as half of people willing to spend up to this amount <marks>A1</marks>

- Recognizes skewed distribution with long whisker from $4$ to $10$ and explains <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the Central Tendency (Median)
steps.content:
First, we look at the median to understand the central tendency of the data. The problem states that the median is $3$.

In the context of the data, the median splits the population into two equal halves. This means that $50\%$ of the people surveyed are willing to spend **at most** $3$ dollars on salt.

steps.title: Analyze the Spread (IQR)
steps.content:
Next, we examine the box of the box plot, which represents the Interquartile Range (IQR). The box extends from the first quartile $(Q_1)$ to the third quartile $(Q_3)$.

Using the given values:

$$\text{Box Range} = [Q_1, Q_3] = [2, 4]$$

This indicates that the middle $50\%$ of the people are willing to spend between $2$ and $4$ dollars.

steps.title: Analyze Skewness (Shape)
steps.content:
Finally, we interpret the shape of the distribution by comparing the lengths of the whiskers (the tails).

Left whisker length:
$$Q_1 - \min = 2 - 1 = 1$$

Right whisker length:
$$\max - Q_3 = 10 - 4 = 6$$

Since the right whisker is much longer than the left whisker, the distribution is **positively skewed** (skewed to the right). This implies that while most people are willing to spend small amounts (around $2$-$4$ dollars), there is a long tail of a few people willing to spend up to $10$ dollars.

parts.content: Calculate the mean score of the students.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify midpoints for each class interval:
  - 35-40: $37.5$ 
  - 40-45: $42.5$
  - 45-50: $47.5$
  - 50-55: $52.5$
  - 55-60: $57.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Multiply each midpoint by its frequency:
  - $37.5 \times 8 = 300$
  - $42.5 \times 12 = 510$
  - $47.5 \times 10 = 475$
  - $52.5 \times 6 = 315$
  - $57.5 \times 4 = 230$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Sum all products: $300 + 510 + 475 + 315 + 230 = 1830$ then divide by total frequency (40): $\frac{1830}{40} = 45.75$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the method
steps.content:
To find the mean of grouped data (data organized in intervals), we assume all values in an interval are located at the midpoint of that interval. We use the formula:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} $$

Where:
- $f_i$ is the frequency of each interval
- $x_i$ is the midpoint of each interval
- $\sum f_i$ is the total number of students

gdc.instructions: Enter (Lower + Upper) / 2 for each interval

ti84.instructions: Enter (Lower + Upper) / 2 for each interval

steps.title: Multiply frequency by midpoint
steps.content:
Multiply each midpoint ($x_i$) by its corresponding frequency ($f_i$) to find the total score contribution for that group:

$$\begin{aligned} 37.5 \times 8 &= 300 \\ 42.5 \times 12 &= 510 \\ 47.5 \times 10 &= 475 \\ 52.5 \times 6 &= 315 \\ 57.5 \times 4 &= 230 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply each midpoint by its frequency

desmos.instructions: Multiply each midpoint by its frequency

steps.title: Sum the products
steps.content:
Add all the products together to get the numerator of our formula ($\sum f_i x_i$):

$$ 300 + 510 + 475 + 315 + 230 = 1830 $$

ti84.instructions: Add the results of the multiplications

desmos.instructions: Add the results of the multiplications

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Finally, divide the sum of products by the total frequency (total number of students).

The total frequency is given as 40.

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{1830}{40} = 45.75 $$

The mean score is **45.75**.

steps.title: Find interval midpoints
steps.content:
Since the data is given in grouped intervals (e.g., $35 - 40$), we need a single value to represent each group. We calculate the **midpoint** for each interval by averaging the lower and upper bounds.

$$\text{Midpoint} = \frac{\text{Lower Bound} + \text{Upper Bound}}{2}$$

- $35 - 40$: $\frac{35+40}{2} = 37.5$
- $40 - 45$: $\frac{40+45}{2} = 42.5$
- $45 - 50$: $\frac{45+50}{2} = 47.5$
- $50 - 55$: $\frac{50+55}{2} = 52.5$
- $55 - 60$: $\frac{55+60}{2} = 57.5$

- **List 2 (Frequency):** $8, 12, 10, 6, 4$

parts.content: Explain what the mean score indicates about the class's performance compared to the scale of the standardized test.
parts.markscheme:
- Mean score of 45.75 is below the midpoint (50) of the scale indicates below average performance for the class as a whole <marks>A1</marks>

- Class grades condensed between 35 - 60 mean that the class is overall average (slightly below) and there are no extremely good performing students or extremely poor performing ones, everyone's towards the middle. <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Allow other valid points</note>

steps.title: Determine the scale midpoint
steps.content:
First, we identify the theoretical average of the test. Since the test is on a scale from 0 to 100, the midpoint represents the standard "average" score.

$$\text{Midpoint} = \frac{0 + 100}{2} = 50$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (0+100)/2

ti84.instructions: Calculate (0+100)/2

calculator.description: Calculate the midpoint of the scale

steps.title: Compare mean to midpoint
steps.content:
Next, compare the class's calculated mean score to this theoretical midpoint. We see that the mean is less than the midpoint:

$$45.75 < 50$$

steps.title: Interpret the result
steps.content:
Since the mean score is below the midpoint of 50, this indicates that the class's performance is **slightly below average** compared to the test scale.

However, because 45.75 is reasonably close to 50, the performance can be described as generally average, just leaning toward the lower end.

parts.content: From the bottom two grade intervals, 10 students were removed from the class, and the average became 48. Determine how many students were removed from each interval.
parts.markscheme:
- Let $x$ = number of students removed from 35-40 interval and $y$ = number of students removed from 40-45 interval where $x + y = 10$ (total students removed) hence we can say $x$ removed from 35-40 interval and $10-x$ removed from 40-45 interval <marks>A1</marks>

- Input new frequencies and values into the mean equation:
$$\frac{37.5(8-x) + 42.5(12-(10-x)) + 47.5(10) + 52.5(6) + 57.5(4)}{30} = 48$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Hence we have $\frac{1830-425+5x}{30}=\frac{1405+5x}{30}=48$ such that $1440 = 1405+5x$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $x=7$ and thus $y=10-x=3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Therefore:
  - 7 students removed from 35-40 interval
  - 3 students removed from 40-45 interval

<note>Award full marks for correct alternative methods that arrive at the same solution</note>

steps.title: Define variables and midpoints
steps.content:
First, we identify the class midpoints for each interval by averaging the bounds (e.g., $\frac{35+40}{2} = 37.5$). 

We are told that **10 students were removed** from the bottom two intervals (35-40 and 40-45). Let $x$ be the number of students removed from the first interval (35-40). Since the total removed is 10, the number removed from the second interval (40-45) is $(10-x)$.

This gives us the new frequencies:
- Interval 35-40: Initial 8 $\rightarrow$ New frequency: $8 - x$
- Interval 40-45: Initial 12 $\rightarrow$ New frequency: $12 - (10-x)$

steps.title: Set up the mean equation
steps.content:
The new average is 48, and the total number of students remaining is $40 - 10 = 30$. Using the formula for the mean of grouped data $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f x}{\sum f}$, we substitute the new frequencies directly into the equation:

$$ \frac{37.5(8-x) + 42.5(12-(10-x)) + 47.5(10) + 52.5(6) + 57.5(4)}{30} = 48 $$

steps.title: Simplify the numerator
steps.content:
We simplify the terms in the numerator. 
For the 40-45 interval term: $12 - (10-x) = 2 + x$.

Now expand the expression:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Sum} &= 37.5(8) - 37.5x + 42.5(2) + 42.5x + 475 + 315 + 230 \\ &= 300 - 37.5x + 85 + 42.5x + 1020 \end{aligned}$$

Grouping the constant terms ($300 + 85 + 1020$) and the $x$ terms ($-37.5x + 42.5x$):
$$ \text{Sum} = 1405 + 5x $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the sum of the static frequency values and the constants from the expanded terms.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the sum of the static frequency values and the constants from the expanded terms.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the sum of the static frequency values and the constants from the expanded terms.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the constant terms

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Now we substitute the simplified sum back into the mean equation and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1405 + 5x}{30} &= 48 \\ 1405 + 5x &= 1440 \\ 5x &= 35 \\ x &= 7 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the mean by the total number of students, subtract the constant sum, and divide by the coefficient of x.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the mean by the total number of students, subtract the constant sum, and divide by the coefficient of x.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the mean by the total number of students, subtract the constant sum, and divide by the coefficient of x.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
We found $x=7$, which represents the number of students removed from the 35-40 interval.
The number of students removed from the 40-45 interval is $10 - x = 10 - 7 = 3$.

**Answer:**
- **7 students** removed from the 35-40 interval
- **3 students** removed from the 40-45 interval

steps.title: Define variables for removed students
steps.content:
First, let's identify the unknowns we need to find. The question asks for the number of students removed from the **bottom two grade intervals** (35-40 and 40-45).

Let:
- $x$ = number of students removed from the 35-40 interval
- $y$ = number of students removed from the 40-45 interval

We are told that a total of **10 students were removed**.

steps.title: Calculate original class total
steps.content:
To find the sum of the scores that were removed, we first need the total score of the entire class before any students were removed. We calculate this using the formula for the mean of grouped data:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{n} $$

First, we find the midpoint ($x_i$) for each interval (e.g., $\frac{35+40}{2} = 37.5$) and multiply by its frequency:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Original Sum} &= 37.5(8) + 42.5(12) + 47.5(10) + 52.5(6) + 57.5(4) \\ &= 300 + 510 + 475 + 315 + 230 \\ &= 1830 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation in the run-matrix or main menu:

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation directly on the home screen:

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new line:

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of frequencies times midpoints

steps.title: Calculate new class total
steps.content:
After removing 10 students, the class size becomes $40 - 10 = 30$ students. We are given that the new **average became 48**.

We can calculate the new total sum of scores remaining in the class:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New Sum} &= \text{New Mean} \times \text{New Count} \\ &= 48 \times 30 \\ &= 1440 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine sum of removed scores
steps.content:
The difference between the original total and the new total represents the sum of the scores of the 10 removed students.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Removed Sum} &= \text{Original Sum} - \text{New Sum} \\ &= 1830 - 1440 \\ &= 390 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up system of equations
steps.content:
Now we can create a system of two linear equations using our variables $x$ and $y$ and the midpoints of the bottom two intervals ($37.5$ and $42.5$).

1. **Equation for quantity:** The total number of removed students is 10.
   $$x + y = 10$$

2. **Equation for sum:** The sum of their scores is 390.
   $$37.5x + 42.5y = 390$$

steps.title: Solve the system
steps.content:
We can solve this system by substitution. From the first equation, we know $y = 10 - x$. Substitute this into the second equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 37.5x + 42.5(10 - x) &= 390 \\ 37.5x + 425 - 42.5x &= 390 \\ -5x + 425 &= 390 \\ -5x &= 390 - 425 \\ -5x &= -35 \\ x &= 7 \end{aligned}$$

Now find $y$:
$$y = 10 - 7 = 3$$

Use the equation solver:

2. Unknowns: 2
3. Enter:
   1x + 1y = 10
   37.5x + 42.5y = 390
4. Press Solve

1. Apps > PlySmlt2 > Simultaneous Eq Solver
2. Set unknowns to 2
3. Enter coefficients:

   37.5x + 42.5y = 390
4. Press SOLVE

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The values we found correspond to the number of students removed from each interval.

- **7 students** were removed from the 35-40 interval.
- **3 students** were removed from the 40-45 interval.

steps.title: Identify values from the plot
steps.content:
First, let's identify the key statistics from the box and whisker plot diagram and the variable labels provided in the question:

*   **Minimum value ($x_{min}$):** 4 (left whisker end)
*   **Median ($Q_2$):** 70 (line inside the box)
*   **Upper Quartile ($Q_3$):** $A$ (right edge of the box)
*   **Maximum value ($x_{max}$):** $B$ (right whisker end)

This question relates to **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**, specifically analyzing box and whisker plots.

steps.title: Calculate B using range
steps.content:
We are given that the range is 266. The range is defined as the difference between the maximum and minimum values.

$$
\text{Range} = x_{max} - x_{min}
$$

Substituting our known values:

$$
\begin{aligned} 266 &= B - 4 \\ B &= 266 + 4 \\ B &= 270 \end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned} 29(A - 70) &= 11(270 - A) \\ 29A - 2030 &= 2970 - 11A \\ 29A + 11A &= 2970 + 2030 \\ 40A &= 5000 \end{aligned}
$$

parts.markscheme:

steps.title: Analyze the region below 4
steps.content:
First, we look at the box and whisker plot to identify the position of the value 4. Based on the plot structure, 4 corresponds to the minimum value (the end of the left whisker). By definition, there are no data points lower than the minimum value.

$$
 P(\text{MMS} < 4) = 0
$$

steps.title: Analyze the region above A
steps.content:
Next, we identify the position of $A$. In a standard box plot, the right side of the box represents the Upper Quartile ($Q_3$). The data falling between the Upper Quartile and the maximum value (the right whisker) always represents exactly $25\%$ (or 0.25) of the total data set.

$$
 P(\text{MMS} > A) = 0.25
$$

steps.title: Sum the probabilities
steps.content:
Since the question asks for the probability that the MMS is either below 4 **or** above $A$, we add the probabilities of these two mutually exclusive regions.

$$
 P(\text{Total}) = P(\text{Below 4}) + P(\text{Above } A)
$$

steps.content:
Substituting the values we found:

$$
 P(\text{Total}) = 0 + 0.25 = 0.25
$$

The probability is 0.25.

steps.title: Identify the complementary event
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that a selected firm has an MMS **below 4 or above $A$**. 

Using the **Complementary Probability** approach, we can find the probability of the opposite event (the complement) and subtract it from 1. The complement event is that the MMS lies **between 4 and $A$**.

steps.title: Analyze the box plot features
steps.content:
By observing the provided box and whisker plot, we can identify what the values 4 and $A$ represent:
- The value **4** is located at the far left end of the whisker, which represents the **Minimum** value of the dataset.
- The value **$A$** is located at the right edge of the box, which represents the **Upper Quartile ($Q_3$)**.

steps.title: Calculate probability of complement
steps.content:
A standard box plot divides data into four sections, each containing 25% (or 0.25) of the data. The interval from the Minimum to the Upper Quartile ($Q_3$) spans three of these sections:
1. Minimum to Lower Quartile ($Q_1$)
2. Lower Quartile ($Q_1$) to Median
3. Median to Upper Quartile ($Q_3$)

We sum these probabilities to find the probability of the complement:
$$
 P(4 \le \text{MMS} \le A) = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.75
$$

steps.title: Calculate final probability
steps.content:
Finally, we subtract the probability of the complement from 1 to find the probability of the original event:

$$
 \begin{aligned} P(\text{below } 4 \cup \text{above } A) &= 1 - P(4 \le \text{MMS} \le A) \\ &= 1 - 0.75 \\ &= 0.25 \end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the probability is **0.25**.

parts.content: Now assume everytime you select one piece of paper, you read the MMS and put it back into the pile such that the probabilities stay the same throughout. If you are to do this 3 times, what is the probability that you get an MMS score above $A$ at least once.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize binomial distribution: MMS score above $A$ has probability $p=0.25$. Let $X$ be the number of scores above $A$. Then $X\sim B(3, 0.25)$. We want to find $\mathbb{P}(X\geq1)$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Use complement rule: Probability of getting at least 1 success is $1-\mathbb{P}(X=0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate probability of zero successes: $\mathbb{P}(X=0)=(0.75)^3 \approx 0.422$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate final probability: $1-0.421875 \approx 0.578$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Determine probability from the plot
steps.content:
First, we must determine the probability of selecting an MMS score above $A$. 

In a box and whisker plot, the box represents the Interquartile Range (IQR). The right edge of the box, labeled $A$, represents the Upper Quartile (Q3). By definition, $25\%$ of the data lies above the Upper Quartile.

Therefore, the probability ($p$) of obtaining a score above $A$ in a single trial is:

$$
 p = 0.25
$$

steps.title: Define the complement event
steps.content:
We are selecting a score 3 times and want to find the probability of getting a score above $A$ **at least once**.

Instead of calculating the probability for getting exactly 1, 2, or 3 scores above $A$, it is much faster to use the **Complement Rule**. The complement of "at least one success" is "zero successes" (none of the scores are above $A$).

$$
 \mathbb{P}(\text{at least one}) = 1 - \mathbb{P}(\text{none})
$$

steps.title: Calculate probability of zero successes
steps.content:
If the probability of success (score $> A$) is $0.25$, then the probability of failure (score $\leq A$) is:

$$
 1 - 0.25 = 0.75
$$

Since the selections are independent (probabilities stay the same), the probability of getting zero successes in 3 trials is:

$$
 \begin{aligned} \mathbb{P}(X=0) &= (0.75)^3 \\ \mathbb{P}(X=0) &= 0.421875 \end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Type 0.75^3 and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.75, press [^], enter 3, then press [ENTER]

steps.title: Apply complement rule
steps.content:
Now, subtract the probability of zero successes from 1 to find the final answer:

$$
 \begin{aligned} \mathbb{P}(X \geq 1) &= 1 - \mathbb{P}(X=0) \\ &= 1 - 0.421875 \\ &= 0.578125 \end{aligned}
$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get:

$$
 \mathbb{P}(X \geq 1) \approx 0.578
$$

ti84.instructions: Type 1 - [2nd] [(-)] (Ans) and press [ENTER]

steps.title: Identify the distribution
steps.content:
First, we identify the type of probability distribution. Since we are selecting a piece of paper, reading it, and putting it back, the probabilities remain constant, and the trials are independent. This describes a **Binomial Distribution** $X \sim B(n, p)$.

*   **Number of trials ($n$):** We select a paper 3 times, so $n = 3$.
*   **Probability of success ($p$):** The question asks for a score "above $A$". In a box and whisker plot, the point $A$ typically represents the upper quartile ($Q_3$), which means $25\%$ of the data lies above it. Therefore, the probability of success is $p = 0.25$.

steps.title: Define the target probability
steps.content:
We want to find the probability of getting a score above $A$ **at least once**. In probability notation, if $X$ is the number of successes, we are looking for $P(X \ge 1)$.

Using the **Summing Probabilities** approach, we need to calculate and add the probabilities for every outcome where $X$ is 1 or greater:
$$
 P(X \ge 1) = P(X=1) + P(X=2) + P(X=3)
$$

steps.title: Calculate P(X = 1)
steps.content:
Using the binomial probability formula $P(X=r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r (1-p)^{n-r}$, we calculate the probability of exactly 1 success.

Here $n=3$, $r=1$, $p=0.25$, and $(1-p)=0.75$:

$$
 \begin{aligned} P(X=1) &= \binom{3}{1} (0.25)^1 (0.75)^{3-1} \\ &= 3 \times 0.25 \times (0.75)^2 \\ &= 0.421875 \end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value directly:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value directly:

steps.title: Calculate P(X = 2)
steps.content:
Now we calculate the probability of exactly 2 successes ($r=2$):

$$
 \begin{aligned} P(X=2) &= \binom{3}{2} (0.25)^2 (0.75)^{3-2} \\ &= 3 \times (0.25)^2 \times (0.75)^1 \\ &= 0.140625 \end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value directly:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value directly:

steps.title: Calculate P(X = 3)
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the probability of exactly 3 successes ($r=3$):

$$
 \begin{aligned} P(X=3) &= \binom{3}{3} (0.25)^3 (0.75)^{3-3} \\ &= 1 \times (0.25)^3 \times 1 \\ &= 0.015625 \end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value directly:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value directly:

steps.title: Sum the probabilities
steps.content:
To find the total probability of at least one success, we sum the individual probabilities we just calculated:

$$
 \begin{aligned} P(X \ge 1) &= 0.421875 + 0.140625 + 0.015625 \\ &= 0.578125 \end{aligned}
$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the probability is **0.578**.

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters
steps.content:
Since we are selecting a piece of paper, reading it, and putting it back (sampling with replacement), the probabilities remain constant. This is a binomial distribution $X \sim B(n, p)$.

*   **Number of trials ($n$):** The process is repeated 3 times, so $n = 3$.
*   **Probability of success ($p$):** In a box and whisker plot, the upper whisker represents the top 25% of the data. Therefore, the probability of getting a score "above $A$" (where $A$ represents the upper quartile $Q_3$) is $p = 0.25$.

steps.title: Set up the cumulative probability
steps.content:
We need to find the probability of getting a score above $A$ **at least once**, which is $P(X \ge 1)$.

Most graphing calculators use the Binomial Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) to calculate $P(X \le k)$. To find $P(X \ge 1)$, we use the complement rule:

$$
 P(X \ge 1) = 1 - P(X \le 0)
$$

Use the `binomcdf` function on your calculator to evaluate $1 - P(X \le 0)$.

*   **Trials ($n$):** 3
*   **p:** 0.25
*   **x value:** 0

$$
 1 - \text{binomcdf}(3, 0.25, 0) \approx 1 - 0.421875
$$

steps.title: Calculate mid-interval values
steps.content:
Since the data is grouped into intervals (e.g., $200 \le t < 240$), we must first find the mid-interval value (midpoint) to represent each group. We calculate this by averaging the lower and upper bounds of each interval.

Midpoints ($x$):
- $\frac{200+240}{2} = 220$
- $\frac{240+280}{2} = 260$
- $\frac{280+320}{2} = 300$
- $\frac{320+360}{2} = 340$
- $\frac{360+400}{2} = 380$
- $\frac{400+440}{2} = 420$

steps.content:

steps.title: Find mid-interval values
steps.content:
To work with grouped data, we first need to represent each interval by a single value. We use the **mid-interval value** (or midpoint) for each class, calculated as the average of the upper and lower boundaries.

$$ \text{Midpoint} = \frac{\text{Lower Bound} + \text{Upper Bound}}{2} $$

- $200 \le t < 240$: $\frac{200+240}{2} = 220$
- $240 \le t < 280$: $\frac{240+280}{2} = 260$
- $280 \le t < 320$: $\frac{280+320}{2} = 300$
- $320 \le t < 360$: $\frac{320+360}{2} = 340$
- $360 \le t < 400$: $\frac{360+400}{2} = 380$
- $400 \le t < 440$: $\frac{400+440}{2} = 420$

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
The mean of grouped data is the sum of the products of frequencies and midpoints ($\,\sum fx\,$), divided by the total frequency ($\,\sum f = n\,$).

First, calculate $\sum fx$:
$$\begin{aligned} \sum fx &= 6(220) + 11(260) + 25(300) + 31(340) + 21(380) + 6(420) \\ &= 1320 + 2860 + 7500 + 10540 + 7980 + 2520 \\ &= 32\,720 \end{aligned}$$

Now divide by the sample size $n = 100$:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{32\,720}{100} = 327.2 \text{ h}$$

steps.title: Calculate sum of squared deviations
steps.content:
To find the standard deviation, we first determine the sum of the squared deviations from the mean for each group, weighted by frequency: $\sum f(x - \bar{x})^2$.

Using $\bar{x} = 327.2$:
$$\begin{aligned} \sum f(x - \bar{x})^2 &= 6(220 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 11(260 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 25(300 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 31(340 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 21(380 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 6(420 - 327.2)^2 \end{aligned}$$

Calculating these terms:
$$ = 68\,951.04 + 49\,674.24 + 18\,496 + 5\,079.04 + 58\,544.64 + 51\,671.04 $$
$$ = 252\,416 $$

steps.title: Calculate standard deviation
steps.content:
Since we are using a sample to **estimate** the population standard deviation, we use the unbiased estimator formula which divides by $n-1$ (instead of $n$).

$$ s_{n-1} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum f(x - \bar{x})^2}{n - 1}} $$

Substitute our values ($n=100$, so $n-1=99$):

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \sqrt{\frac{252\,416}{99}} \\ &= \sqrt{2549.656...} \\ &\approx 50.5 \text{ h} \end{aligned}$$

(Note: If you used $n=100$, you would get $50.2$, but for sample estimates, $n-1$ is the correct method.)

parts.content: For Sample A, construct a cumulative-frequency table and estimate the median, $Q_1$, $Q_3$ and IQR (linear interpolation within classes).
parts.markscheme:
- Correct cumulative frequencies: $6, 17, 42, 73, 94, 100$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Valid attempt to use linear interpolation formula for median or quartiles. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\text{Median} \approx 330\text{ h}$ (accept $330.3$). <marks>A1</marks>
- $Q_1 \approx 293\text{ h}$ ($292.8$) and $Q_3 \approx 364\text{ h}$ ($363.8$). <marks>A1 A1</marks>
- $\text{IQR} \approx 71.0\text{ h}$. <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Construct cumulative frequency table
steps.content:
First, we construct the cumulative frequency table by adding the frequencies cumulatively for each interval.

*   $200 \le t < 240$: $6$
*   $240 \le t < 280$: $6 + 11 = 17$
*   $280 \le t < 320$: $17 + 25 = 42$
*   $320 \le t < 360$: $42 + 31 = 73$
*   $360 \le t < 400$: $73 + 21 = 94$
*   $400 \le t < 440$: $94 + 6 = 100$

The cumulative frequencies are: **6, 17, 42, 73, 94, 100**.

steps.title: Method for linear interpolation
steps.content:
To estimate the quartiles and median from grouped data, we use the linear interpolation formula:

$$ \text{Value} = L + \left( \frac{P - F_{before}}{f_{class}} \right) \times w $$

Where:
*   $L$ is the lower boundary of the class interval
*   $P$ is the position of the required statistic (e.g., $25^{th}$ value)
*   $F_{before}$ is the cumulative frequency of the previous interval
*   $f_{class}$ is the frequency of the current interval
*   $w$ is the class width

steps.title: Estimate the median
steps.content:
The median is the $50\%$ position. Since $N=100$, we look for the $50^{th}$ value.

Looking at the cumulative frequencies, the $50^{th}$ value falls in the interval $320 \le t < 360$ (since $42 < 50 \le 73$).

*   $L = 320$
*   $P = 50$
*   $F_{before} = 42$
*   $f_{class} = 31$
*   $w = 360 - 320 = 40$

Substituting these into the formula:

$$ \text{Median} = 320 + \left( \frac{50 - 42}{31} \right) \times 40 $$

$$ \text{Median} = 320 + \left( \frac{8}{31} \right) \times 40 \approx 330.32 $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the interpolation expression

ti84.instructions: Calculate the interpolation expression

steps.title: Estimate the first quartile (Q1)
steps.content:
$Q_1$ is the $25\%$ position. We look for the $25^{th}$ value.

This falls in the interval $280 \le t < 320$ (since $17 < 25 \le 42$).

*   $L = 280$
*   $P = 25$
*   $F_{before} = 17$
*   $f_{class} = 25$
*   $w = 40$

$$ Q_1 = 280 + \left( \frac{25 - 17}{25} \right) \times 40 $$

$$ Q_1 = 280 + \left( \frac{8}{25} \right) \times 40 = 280 + 12.8 = 292.8 $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the interpolation expression

ti84.instructions: Calculate the interpolation expression

steps.title: Estimate the third quartile (Q3)
steps.content:
$Q_3$ is the $75\%$ position. We look for the $75^{th}$ value.

This falls in the interval $360 \le t < 400$ (since $73 < 75 \le 94$).

*   $L = 360$
*   $P = 75$
*   $F_{before} = 73$
*   $f_{class} = 21$
*   $w = 40$

$$ Q_3 = 360 + \left( \frac{75 - 73}{21} \right) \times 40 $$

$$ Q_3 = 360 + \left( \frac{2}{21} \right) \times 40 \approx 363.81 $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the interpolation expression

ti84.instructions: Calculate the interpolation expression

steps.title: Calculate the IQR
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the Interquartile Range (IQR) by subtracting $Q_1$ from $Q_3$.

$$ \text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 $$

$$ \text{IQR} \approx 363.81 - 292.8 = 71.01 $$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:
*   Median $\approx 330$ h
*   $Q_1 \approx 293$ h
*   $Q_3 \approx 364$ h
*   IQR $\approx 71.0$ h

- Lower bound $= Q_1 - (1.5 \times 71.0) \approx 186$ ($186.3$). <marks>A1</marks>
- Upper bound $= Q_3 + (1.5 \times 71.0) \approx 470$ ($470.3$).

- All Sample A values are within $[200, 440) \Rightarrow$ no outliers expected. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Retrieve quartiles and IQR
steps.content:

- $$Q_1 ≈ 292.8$$

These are the values we will use to find the outlier boundaries.

steps.title: Calculate the outlier step
steps.content:
The question asks us to use $$1.5 \times \text{IQR}$$ to find the outlier bounds. Let's calculate this value first:

$$1.5 \times 71.0 = 106.5$$

steps.title: Find the lower boundary
steps.content:
An outlier is any value smaller than the lower boundary. We calculate this by subtracting the step from the first quartile:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Lower Boundary} &= Q_1 - (1.5 \times \text{IQR}) \\ &= 292.8 - 106.5 \\ &= 186.3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the upper boundary
steps.content:
An outlier is also any value larger than the upper boundary. We calculate this by adding the step to the third quartile:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Upper Boundary} &= Q_3 + (1.5 \times \text{IQR}) \\ &= 363.8 + 106.5 \\ &= 470.3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Compare with sample data
steps.content:
Finally, we check if any batteries in Sample A fall outside the interval $$[186.3, 470.3]$$. Looking at the frequency table:

- The minimum lifetime is $$200$$ h (start of the first interval).
- The maximum lifetime is less than $$440$$ h (end of the last interval).

Since $$200 > 186.3$$ and $$440 < 470.3$$, all data values are within the calculated boundaries.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content: Since all values in the sample lie between the lower and upper boundaries, no outliers are expected.

parts.content: Give two reasons why Sample B should estimate the population mean more reliably than Sample A.
parts.markscheme:
- Award <marks>1</marks> for each valid reason. Max <marks>2</marks>.

Examples:
- Stratified sampling (by day) mitigates day/shift effects.
- Averages process variation across days; Sample A may be biased by one shift.
- Random selection each day reduces convenience/cluster bias found in Sample A.

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the sampling designs
steps.content:
First, let's identify the structure of each sample to understand their differences in reliability.

*   **Sample A** takes 100 batteries from a **single shift**. This is a form of cluster sampling where the cluster is one specific time period. It assumes that this one shift is perfectly representative of all production.
*   **Sample B** takes 10 batteries from **10 consecutive days**. This is a form of stratified sampling (stratified by time), where the sample is spread out over a longer period.

highlights.text: 10 batteries at random at the end of each of 10 consecutive days

steps.title: Reason 1: Reducing single-shift bias
steps.content:
The first reason Sample B is more reliable is that it mitigates anomalies associated with a specific time. 

Sample A is taken from a single shift. If there was a temporary issue during that specific shift—such as a machine calibration error, a different worker, or unusual temperature—the entire sample of 100 would be biased. Sample B minimizes this risk because an anomaly on one day would only affect 10% of the data, not 100%.

highlights.text: Sample A may be biased by one shift

steps.title: Reason 2: Capturing process variation
steps.content:
The second reason is that Sample B better captures the natural variation of the manufacturing process.

Manufacturing processes often fluctuate slightly from day to day. By spreading the sample over **10 consecutive days**, Sample B averages out these daily fluctuations, leading to an estimate that is more representative of the overall population mean compared to Sample A, which only sees the variation within a few hours.

parts.content: Calculate the mean distance of the students hiding.
parts.markscheme:
- Find the midpoints: $\{10,30,50,70,90\}$ and products $f \cdot x$: $\{100,450,750,350,450\}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate totals: $n=\sum f = 50$ and $\sum (f \cdot x) = 2100$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate mean: $\text{Mean} = \frac{2100}{50} = 42$ metres <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the mean of grouped data, we use the formula:

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$$

Where:
- $f_i$ is the frequency of each interval
- $x_i$ is the midpoint of each interval
- $\sum$ represents the sum of the values

This concept is part of **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**, specifically calculating the **Mean of grouped data**.

steps.title: Find the midpoints
steps.content:
For grouped data, we assume all data points in an interval are located at the midpoint ($x$). Calculate the midpoint for each distance interval by averaging the lower and upper bounds:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 \text{ to } 20 &\rightarrow \dfrac{0+20}{2} = 10 \\ 20 \text{ to } 40 &\rightarrow \dfrac{20+40}{2} = 30 \\ 40 \text{ to } 60 &\rightarrow \dfrac{40+60}{2} = 50 \\ 60 \text{ to } 80 &\rightarrow \dfrac{60+80}{2} = 70 \\ 80 \text{ to } 100 &\rightarrow \dfrac{80+100}{2} = 90 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Multiply frequency by midpoint
steps.content:
Multiply the frequency ($f$) of each interval by its midpoint ($x$) to find the total distance contribution for that group ($f \cdot x$):

$$\begin{aligned} 10 \times 10 &= 100 \\ 15 \times 30 &= 450 \\ 15 \times 50 &= 750 \\ 5 \times 70 &= 350 \\ 5 \times 90 &= 450 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product of frequency and midpoint for each row.

ti84.instructions: Enter 10*10, 15*30, etc. to verify each product.

steps.title: Calculate the totals
steps.content:
Sum the frequencies ($\sum f$) and the products ($\sum f x$) to substitute into our formula.

Total Frequency:
$$\sum f = 10 + 15 + 15 + 5 + 5 = 50$$

Sum of Products:
$$\sum f x = 100 + 450 + 750 + 350 + 450 = 2100$$

gdc.instructions: Sum the products: 100+450+750+350+450

ti84.instructions: Sum the individual products calculated in the previous step.

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Divide the sum of the products by the total frequency to find the estimated mean distance.

$$\text{Mean} = \dfrac{2100}{50}$$ 

$$\text{Mean} = 42 \text{ metres}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2100/50

ti84.instructions: Divide the sum of products by the total frequency.

parts.content: The seeker has to catch the same number of people from each class interval. If you get caught, you are out. The seeker caught $k$ people from each interval such that the mean distance of those who are still in the game is 40. Find $k$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the new frequencies $f_i - k$ and the mean equation:
$$ \frac{\sum (f_i - k) x_i}{\sum (f_i - k)} = 40 $$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute values:
$$ \frac{2100 - 250k}{50 - 5k} = 40 $$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Cross multiply and expand:
$$ \begin{aligned} 2100 - 250k &= 40(50 - 5k) \\ 2100 - 250k &= 2000 - 200k \end{aligned} $$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $k$:
$$ 100 = 50k $$
$$ k = 2 $$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.content:
This question asks us to draw a frequency histogram for the remaining students. From the previous parts of the question (or by comparing the initial total of 50 students to the remaining total of 40 shown in the markscheme), we know that $k = 2$ students were caught from each interval.

A histogram is used for continuous data, so we must draw bars that touch each other (no gaps) with the correct frequency heights.

steps.title: Calculate remaining frequencies
steps.content:
We calculate the new frequency for each interval by subtracting $k=2$ from the initial frequencies (which were 10, 15, 15, 5, and 5). 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{0-20 m:} && 10 - 2 &= 8 \\ \text{20-40 m:} && 15 - 2 &= 13 \\ \text{40-60 m:} && 15 - 2 &= 13 \\ \text{60-80 m:} && 5 - 2 &= 3 \\ \text{80-100 m:} && 5 - 2 &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

Plot the bars using the calculated heights. Remember that for a histogram representing continuous data, the bars must be **continuous** (touching each other).

*   **0–20:** Height 8

Ensure each bar has a width of 20 units on the x-axis.

steps.title: Determine the class intervals
steps.content:
This question relates to **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**, specifically **SL 4.2** on Cumulative Frequency graphs.

The question asks for a table with **4 equal class intervals**. First, we identify the total range of the time ($t$) from the x-axis of the graph, which goes from 0 to 160.

We calculate the width of each interval:

$$\text{Width} = \frac{\text{Maximum Time}}{\text{Number of Intervals}} = \frac{160}{4} = 40$$

The four intervals are:
- $0 < t \leq 40$
- $40 < t \leq 80$
- $80 < t \leq 120$
- $120 < t \leq 160$

steps.title: Read cumulative frequencies
steps.content:
Using the **Cumulative Frequency Differences** method, we first read the cumulative frequency ($CF$) value at the upper boundary of each interval directly from the graph curve.

- At $t = 40$: The curve is just above 0. Let's read this as $$CF(40) = 1$$.
- At $t = 80$: The curve is just below 10. Let's read this as $$CF(80) = 9$$.
- At $t = 120$: The curve is above 30. Let's read this as $$CF(120) = 33$$.
- At $t = 160$: The curve reaches the maximum. $$CF(160) = 40$$.

steps.title: Calculate interval frequencies
steps.content:
The frequency for each interval is found by subtracting the cumulative frequency of the previous interval from the current one.

**Interval $0 < t \leq 40$:**
$$\text{Frequency} = CF(40) - CF(0) = 1 - 0 = 1$$

**Interval $40 < t \leq 80$:**
$$\text{Frequency} = CF(80) - CF(40) = 9 - 1 = 8$$

**Interval $80 < t \leq 120$:**
$$\text{Frequency} = CF(120) - CF(80) = 33 - 9 = 24$$

**Interval $120 < t \leq 160$:**
$$\text{Frequency} = CF(160) - CF(120) = 40 - 33 = 7$$

steps.title: Construct the final table
steps.content:
Now we arrange these values into the frequency table. As a final check, verify that the frequencies sum to the total number of balls:

$$1 + 8 + 24 + 7 = 40$$

| Time Interval ($t$) | Frequency |
| :---: | :---: |
| $0 < t \leq 40$ | 1 |
| $40 < t \leq 80$ | 8 |
| $80 < t \leq 120$ | 24 |
| $120 < t \leq 160$ | 7 |
